common_name: Triple Curled Parsley (Dense Dark-Green Garnish Parsley)

planting.note: Direct sow or start indoors 6–8 weeks before transplanting; parsley seed germinates very slowly (14–28 days) and benefits from soaking in warm water overnight before planting. In zone 9, direct sow September–February for fall through spring production; parsley is a biennial that survives zone 9 winters and produces best in cool weather. Germination is notoriously slow — keep soil consistently moist for up to 4 weeks waiting for seedlings.

directions: Soak seeds overnight before sowing 0.25 inch deep; thin to 8–12 inches. Triple Curled parsley (also called Extra Curled or Triple Moss Curled) is the standard garnish parsley of American restaurant and commercial use — a Petroselinum crispum variety selected for the densest, most tightly packed curling of the leaves, creating a compact, almost moss-like texture in the leaf heads that makes neat, visually attractive garnishes. The triple curling (each leaf curled multiple times rather than the single curl of standard curly parsley) creates a denser, darker green head with more surface area and a more formal, decorative appearance than less-curled types. The flavor of curly parsley types is milder and slightly less complex than flat-leaf Italian parsley — less aromatic oils, less intense flavor — which is why many serious cooks prefer flat-leaf for cooking while curly types dominate in garnish applications where appearance is primary. Triple Curled produces compact, upright plants with excellent visual appeal in both ornamental and kitchen herb gardens. As a biennial, parsley lives through its first winter (when harvested from), sets seed in the second year, and dies — a growth cycle that supports fall planting in zone 9 for winter and spring harvest.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Curly parsley variety comparison
Burpee Seeds — Triple Curled parsley variety notes
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Herb Production Guide

common_name: Common Chervil (Classic French Fines Herbes Anise Herb)

planting.note: Direct sow chervil in place and do not transplant — the taproot is extremely sensitive to disturbance. Sow fresh seed immediately; old seed has poor germination. In zone 9, sow August–October for fall and winter harvest when chervil performs best; spring planting is possible (January–February) but the plants bolt quickly in warming weather. Chervil grows well in partial shade and actually benefits from afternoon shade in zone 9 — use it under taller plants or in a partially shaded bed.

directions: Direct sow fresh seed on or near the soil surface in cool weather; do not cover deeply — chervil needs some light. Do not transplant. Chervil (Anthriscus cerefolium) is one of the four classic herbs of the French fines herbes blend (with parsley, chives, and French tarragon) — a cool-season, shade-tolerant herb with delicate, lacy, bright green leaves resembling a more refined parsley, and a distinctive flavor that combines mild parsley with a clean anise note. The anise quality distinguishes chervil from all other common culinary herbs — it is present but not dominant, providing a refined freshness that enhances eggs, fish, chicken, and light cream sauces without overwhelming the primary ingredients. Chervil is the most perishable of the major culinary herbs: the delicate flavor dissipates rapidly when dried (dried chervil has almost no culinary value), when cooked at high heat (add at the end of cooking), or when refrigerated for more than 2–3 days. These perishability characteristics mean chervil is essentially only available to gardeners — it is rarely found fresh in American markets. Chervil bolt rapidly (runs to seed) in heat, making it strictly a cool-season herb for zone 8–9 gardeners, but the fall-through-spring window in zone 9 provides several months of harvest from a fall planting.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Chervil variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Culinary Herb Production Guide

common_name: Vietnamese Cilantro (Rau Ram Heat-Tolerant Cilantro Substitute)

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings in partial shade to full sun after last frost; Vietnamese cilantro is a tropical perennial that will survive mild zone 9 winters with some frost protection, making it a semi-permanent herb planting in the Gulf South. In zone 9, established plants often survive to grow back in spring unless temperatures drop below 28°F. Stem cuttings root readily in water or moist potting mix. Vietnamese cilantro is one of the few heat-tolerant herbs with a cilantro-like flavor profile, making it valuable in Gulf South summers when true cilantro bolts.

directions: Root 4-inch stem cuttings in water or moist potting mix; transplant to partial shade after last frost. Vietnamese Cilantro (Persicaria odorata, Rau Răm in Vietnamese) is a Southeast Asian tropical herb that produces an aroma remarkably similar to cilantro (Coriandrum sativum) combined with a slightly peppery, more complex note — making it the primary heat-tolerant substitute for cilantro in Gulf South gardens, where true cilantro bolts and becomes bitter in temperatures above 75°F. Vietnamese cilantro is a creeping, semi-aquatic perennial that grows vigorously in warm, moist conditions — it is widely grown in Vietnam and Southeast Asia where it is used fresh in spring rolls (gỏi cuốn), rice paper wraps, laksa (the spicy Southeast Asian noodle soup that uses rau răm as a key garnish), and various dipping sauce accompaniments. The narrow, pointed leaves with distinctive dark V-shaped markings are the visual identifier. In the Gulf South, Vietnamese cilantro solves the common problem of wanting fresh cilantro-flavored herbs in July through September when true cilantro is impossible to grow — it grows vigorously in the summer heat and provides a suitable (though not identical) flavor substitute.
sources: Richters Herbs — Vietnamese cilantro cultivation guide
Johnny's Selected Seeds — Rau Ram variety notes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Herbs for Florida Gardens

common_name: Peter Pan Pattypan Squash (AAS-Winning Green Scallop Summer Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Peter Pan is a bush-type pattypan suitable for smaller gardens than vining summer squash. In zone 9, plant March–May and again August for fall harvest. Harvest at 3–4 inches diameter for best flavor and texture — pattypan squash becomes seedy and watery if allowed to grow much beyond 4–5 inches. The scalloped edges that give pattypan its distinctive star or UFO shape become more pronounced and decorative as fruits approach full harvest size.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep after last frost; harvest fruits at 3–4 inches diameter for best eating quality. Peter Pan is a 1982 All-America Selections-winning pattypan (scallop) summer squash — an early-maturing, bush-habit variety producing pale green, disc-shaped fruits with the characteristic scalloped, fluted edges of the pattypan type. The distinctive flat, disc-like shape with scalloped margins distinguishes pattypan squash from all other summer squash types (zucchini, yellow crookneck, yellow straightneck) and has no equivalent in other vegetable categories — the shape makes individual fruits look like tiny flying saucers or UFOs and creates an unusually attractive presentation on the plate when halved or stuffed whole. Peter Pan's light green color is gentler and less common than the white pattypans that dominated before its introduction. The flavor is mild and slightly sweet — similar to other summer squash but with a distinctive nuttiness that develops in the scallop varieties at the ideal 3–4 inch harvest size. The bush habit (24–36 inch spread vs. the 6–8 foot spread of vining squash) makes Peter Pan practical for smaller raised beds and gardens.
sources: All-America Selections — Peter Pan pattypan squash AAS winner 1982
Burpee Seeds — Peter Pan scallop squash variety notes
University of Georgia Cooperative Extension — Summer Squash Production

common_name: Red Hubbard Squash (Large Traditional Orange-Red Winter Storage Squash)

planting.note: Direct sow after last frost; Red Hubbard requires a long season (92–105 days) and is harvested as a storage squash in fall. In zone 9, plant March–April for September–October harvest. Cure harvested fruits at 80–85°F for 2 weeks to harden the skin before moving to cool, dry storage (50–55°F) where they keep 3–5 months. The very hard shell and warty skin require a heavy, sharp knife or cleaver to cut — many cooks bake the whole squash before cutting.

directions: Direct sow seeds 1 inch deep in hills of 3 seeds after last frost; thin to 2 plants. Cure harvested fruits 2 weeks at warm temperature before cool storage. Red Hubbard is a classic large Cucurbita maxima winter squash — one of the largest commonly grown winter squash types, producing fruits typically weighing 10–15 lbs with distinctive warty, orange-red to brick-red-orange skin and a teardrop to large, irregular form with a knobby, pointed blossom end. The Hubbard class of squash has a long American horticultural history, dating to the mid-19th century when the Blue Hubbard type was brought to New England and became a regional storage squash staple. Red Hubbard is a warmer-colored variant with orange-red skin rather than the Blue Hubbard's steel-blue-gray. The very thick, hard shell provides exceptional storage capability (stored roots keep 4–6 months) and was important in pre-refrigeration households where squash served as an important carbohydrate source through winter. The deep orange flesh is very dense, dry, and sweet — the classic winter squash flavor in concentrated form, excellent for pies, soups, and roasted preparations where the flavor holds up to extended cooking.
sources: Seed Savers Exchange — Red Hubbard squash variety history
Baker Creek Heirloom Seeds — Hubbard squash collection
Cornell Cooperative Extension — Winter Squash Storage and Curing
